comments.text: There seems to be a fairly cheap way to improve the upper bound to $\exp((\log x)^{1/3+o(1)})$. For what it's worth, I have typed up a short note containing the argument. Perhaps this can be pushed further with some new ideas.

This is very nice Adrian! A few comments that may or may not be fruitful: 0) Where you write $\le (\log x)^{1/3}$ in the middle of page $2$, I think this should actually be equality. 1) For the number of possibilities for $f(n)$ you take $v_p(n) \le \log_2(n)$, presumably for simplicity. You are certainly aware of this, but here we could use the fact that $p > |I|/6$ so that $v_p(n) \le \log_{|I|/6}(n) \le (\log x)^{2/3}$. In the end this only changes the implicit constant in the exponent, but it seemed worth mentioning. 2) For the number of possibilities for $g(n)$ you similarly note that $n$ has at most $\log_2(n)$ prime factors. Perhaps using some version of Erdős-Kac in short intervals, an analogue of Lemma $2.1$ still holds where $S$ only contains integers with approximately $\log \log n$ prime factors. Again, this only marginally impacts the final bound (if it even works). But again, I still wanted to point it out ;)

Thanks Wouter for your comments! Regarding 0), you are absolutely right, this is in fact an equality. As for 1), I'm aware of this, but as you said, it doesn't impact the final shape of the bound. Also, it did occur to me that 2) might lead to an improvement, but I'm not sure whether something along these lines is feasible. In particular, it again seems to be difficult to control the behaviour of the large prime factors.
